data.title: Best healthcare REITs ranked by Seeking Alpha Quant ratings
data.description:
Healthcare Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) offer an indirect avenue to benefit from the growth in the healthcare sector as they own or finance income-generating healthcare-related real estate.

From senior living communities to hospitals, medical offices to outpatient facilities, and skilled nursing facilities, healthcare REITs own a wide range of healthcare properties, giving investors exposure to the real estate side of the sector.

Healthcare REITs also offer investors an opportunity to capitalize on the rising Baby Boomer population. Given the advancements in healthcare, those older than 80 are set to become one of the fastest-growing age groups in the years ahead, leading to a concurrent boost in demand for senior housing and skilled nursing facilities, benefiting REITs focused on those properties.

Here is a look at the top 10 healthcare REITs ranked according to Seeking Alpha's Quant System, which has consistently beaten the broader market over the past five years.

  1. Sila Realty Trust (NYSE:SILA): – Quant rating: 4.82
  2. CareTrust REIT (CTRE): – Quant rating: 4.78
  3. Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA): – Quant rating: 4.43
  4. Omega Healthcare Investors (NYSE:OHI): – Quant rating: 4.30
  5. LTC Properties (LTC): – Quant rating: 3.50
  6. Welltower (NYSE:WELL): – Quant rating: 3.47
  7. American Healthcare REIT (AHR): – Quant rating: 3.40
  8. Ventas (NYSE:VTR): – Quant rating: 3.24
  9. Strawberry Fields REIT (STRW): – Quant rating: 3.22
  10. National Health Investors (NHI): – Quant rating: 2.95

data.title: Tariff worries starting to hit business surveys, inflation expectations & home builders
data.description:
Economic data this week was further evidence that the threat of U.S. President Donald Trump's proposed tariffs was starting to hit business surveys, fuel inflation expectations and even make home builders nervous.

Tariff drama has dominated Wall Street's headlines over the past two weeks. The latest development was Trump's remarks on Tuesday about probably announcing 25% tariffs on imports of cars, semiconductors, and pharmaceuticals on April 2.

Earlier, Trump announced then delayed 25% tariffs on imports from Mexico and Canada, and implemented 10% tariffs on imports from China. He also announced 25% levies on all steel and aluminum imports into the U.S. Furthermore, the President has tasked his administration with investigating current U.S. trade relations and proposing reciprocal tariffs on a country-specific basis.

"Over the last few weeks this essay has led with the various tariff threats coming out of the administration. Those threats continued this week. One side effect of this saber-rattling may have manifested itself in this week’s data as a number of business surveys showed a deterioration in the outlook, in some cases directly attributed to the brewing trade conflict," JPMorgan's Michael Feroli said on Friday.

S&P Global's flash U.S. PMI readings on Friday showed new order growth weakening sharply in February and business expectations for the year ahead slumping "amid growing concerns and uncertainty related to federal government policies."

Meanwhile, the headline output index dropped to its lowest level in 17 months to signal a near-stalling of business activity in February, with much of that weakness driven by a first contraction in output in the services sector in just over two years.

In a bright spot, the flash manufacturing output index ticked higher in February. However, S&P Global said that the upturn was "also in part linked to the front-running of tariffs, hinting at merely a temporary boost."

Separately, on Friday, the University of Michigan's final monthly survey of consumers for February showed long-run inflation expectations climbing to 3.5% from 3.2% in January, marking the largest M/M increase since May 2021.

"Trade concerns were also likely a factor in the recent rise in household inflation expectations, as measured by the University of Michigan’s survey of consumers," JPMorgan's Feroli said.

"Longer-term inflation expectations jumped to 3.5% in February, near the highs of the COVID inflation surge after adjusting for recent changes in the survey method. Underscoring the idea that tariffs are an adverse supply shock—bad for both inflation and growth—consumer perceptions of buying conditions also deteriorated markedly last month, after having briefly spiked as people said they were front-loading purchases," the analyst added.

Friday's data contributed in part to a selloff on Wall Street that saw the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) slip more than 2% and the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) post its worst intraday performance since mid-December 2024.

"As focus in these tariff negotiations looks set to shift from border issues to more structural economic issues like reducing persistent trade deficits, protecting certain American industries, and forcing other countries to change their own tax and regulatory policies, the risk that some tariffs may actually be put in place—and potentially for some time—appears to be drifting up. Recent declines in sentiment measures and a late-week sell-off in equity markets suggests growing recognition of these risks," Feroli said.

Tariff concerns have cropped up in home builders as well. Builder confidence in the market in February for newly built single-family homes fell to its lowest level in five months, according to the National Association of Home Builders (NAHB).

"Uncertainty on the tariff front helped push builders’ expectations for future sales volume down to the lowest level since December 2023," Carl Harris, NAHB chairman, said in a statement on Tuesday.

"With 32% of appliances and 30% of softwood lumber coming from international trade, uncertainty over the scale and scope of tariffs has builders further concerned about costs," weighed in Robert Dietz, NAHB chief economist.

"Reflecting this outlook, builder responses collected prior to a pause for the proposed tariffs on goods from Canada and Mexico yielded a lower (housing market index) reading of 38, while those collected after the announced one-month pause produced a score of 44," Dietz added.

Here are some popular exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

Here are some China-linked ETFs: (KWEB), (PGJ), (CQQQ), (FXI), (GXC), (MCHI), (FLCH), (CNYA), (ASHR), and (YINN).

Some Canada-linked ETFs: (EWC), (FXC), (FLCA), and (BBCA).

And some Mexico-linked ones: (EWW), (FLMX), and (MEXX).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Over 88% of the companies that reported earnings this week, delivered EPS wins-Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages were mixed on Friday, as markets looked to regain momentum after Thursday’s sell-off, which was partly sparked by consumer giant Walmart’s disappointing outlook.

U.S. stocks finished with losses Thursday, with the S&P 500 pulling back from a record high, in part as investors balked at Walmart's (WMT) soft financial outlook.

According to Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid, a weaker-than-expected forecast from Walmart added to nerves about the health of the consumer right now, especially after a soft retail sales print last week.

Despite the market’s gloomy front, the holiday-shortened week was marked by an upbeat earnings scorecard with 38 out of 43 companies reporting better-than expected earnings per share, and 30 companies beating revenue estimates.

Looking at sector-wise performance, all five companies from the Basic Materials sector posted better than expected EPS. Prominent names included Newmont Corporation (NEM), which reported Non-GAAP earnings per share of $1.40, beating estimates by 0.36%, on a 42.7% increase in revenue to $5.65B.

Out of the seven consumer sector companies that reported earnings this week, Walmart (WMT), and LKQ Corporation (LKQ) disappointed investors with misses.

Walmart’s outlook for the current quarter and FY26 failed to meet Wall Street’s lofty expectations. Looking ahead to the current quarter and for the full year 2026, the company’s guidance “assumes a generally stable consumer and continued pressure from its mix of products and formats globally," the company cautioned.

The expectation for Q1 sales to be up a 3% to 4% should result in a profit between $0.57 and $0.58 per share. Down 3 to 4 cents from a year ago and below the consensus estimate of $0.65.

Other big names in the sector, such as Booking Holdings (BKNG) reported a double-digit growth in room nights booked, and gross bookings, fuelling a 15% increase in Q4 revenue.

Toy company Hasbro (HAS), another prominent name from the Consumer sector, also had a great inning with a less-than-expected decline in overall holiday sales, helping bolster the company’s bottom-line above what Wall Street expected.

Looking at the Energy sector, three out of four companies that reported earnings this week, beat EPS estimates. While Occidental Petroleum (OXY) beat EPS estimates, it reported revenues of $6.84B, missing estimates by $300M.

Among the four healthcare companies that reported earnings this week, all of them beat EPS estimates, while Medtronic (MDT) missed revenue expectations by $33.58M.

Six companies from the Industrials sector reported earnings this week, with all of them beating EPS expectations, except Nordson Corporation (NDSN) which missed analysts’ estimates for both top and bottom-line numbers.

Meanwhile, the tech sector had another week of outstanding earnings, with both and top and bottom-line beats from all the seven companies in the sector that reported earnings this week.

Arista Networks (ANET) posted fourth-quarter results and issued guidance that were above Wall Street's estimates, due in part to the continued artificial intelligence spending boom.

Meanwhile, six companies from the Utilities sector reported earnings this week, with only one of them missing EPS estimates, and three names missing revenue estimates.

For the upcoming week, more than 45 companies are scheduled to report earnings, with Coterra Energy (CTRA), ONEOK (OKE), and SBA Communications (SBAC) reporting on Monday. Home Depot (HD) and Intuit (INTU) are set to report on Tuesday, while names such as Lowe's (LOW), Nvidia (NVDA), and Salesforce (CRM) will be reporting on Wednesday.

Thursday will include major tech names such as Autodesk (ADSK), Dell Technologies (DELL), and HP Inc (HPQ) among others.

data.title: Lilly, Novo dominance to continue as TD Cowen lifts GLP-1 sales forecast for 2030 by 38%
data.description:
TD Cowen projects that with Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) continuing their dominance, global sales of the GLP-1 class of weight loss/obesity drugs could reach $139B by 2030, indicating more than a 38% increase from its prior forecast.

The brokerage cited Lilly (NYSE:LLY) and Novo’s (OTCPK:NONOF) continuing dominance, a notable interest in GLP-1s from primary care providers, and a rich obesity pipeline from rivals as some of the reasons for the upgrade.

According to TD Cowen, Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) which offer obesity/ diabetes drugs, tirzepatide and semaglutide will be competing neck and neck in the GLP-1 market in 2030 with their products, Mounjaro/ Zepbound and Ozempic/ Wegovy, respectively.

Continuing demand for NVO’s obesity products and swift uptake of LLY’s treatments, “a near flawless clinical trial record as these products add indications, and a rich pipeline suggest that this estimate is achievable,” the analysts wrote.

Clinical trials are underway to test LLY and NVO’s products for additional health conditions, and the U.S. FDA has already approved semaglutide to reduce major adverse cardiovascular events and tirzepatide for obstructive sleep apnea in overweight or obese people.

However, TD Cowen projects diabetes and obesity to account for 59% and 41% of the GLP-1 sales in 2030, respectively, and LLY to take roughly 48% share of the market just ahead of NVO’s 45% share in a market estimated to climb 21% CAGR from 2023.

“LLY and NVO are expected to remain the main drivers and beneficiaries of the market growth. But there are clinical and commercial lanes open to competition, and no dearth of market hopefuls vying for a spot as the diabetes and obesity landscape expands,” TD Cowen wrote.

The firm noted that other obesity drug developers such as Amgen (AMGN), Altimmune (ALT), Viking Therapeutics (VKTX), Regeneron (REGN), AstraZeneca (AZN), Structure Therapeutics (GPCR), Metsera (MTSR), and Terns Pharma (TERN) have posted promising initial trial data.

“However, LLY and NVO are still best positioned in the GLP-1 market and still poised to outperform peers,” TD Cowen concluded.

data.title: Trending stocks in a bearish week for Wall Street
data.description:
The U.S. stock market had one of its worst weeks so far in 2025, weighed down by inflation concerns and weaker-than-expected economic data.

The S&P 500 lost over 1.8%, hitting its lowest end since earlier this month. The Dow Jones Industrial Average slipped 2.9%, while the Nasdaq Composite dropped 2.2%.

The market faltered in reaction to data from the US Composite Flash PMI Output Index, showing a slowdown in economic growth over the past two months.

US manufacturers also reported the highest selling price inflation in two years, with many blaming the upward pressure on prices on tariffs and disruptions from recent US government policies.

In the midst of this gloomy week, these were some of the trending stocks:

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) shares jumped 13%, clocking its third consecutive week of gains and recovering losses from last summer’s critical report alleging accounting violations. The optimism builds as the information technology company nears its Feb. 25 deadline to file its 2024 annual report, along with its quarterly reports for the September 2024 and December 2024 periods.

Carvana (CVNA) shares fell despite reporting better-than-expected fourth quarter results. The used-car retailer reported lower gross profit per vehicle and shrinking wholesale volumes, as per Bloomberg estimates. Morgan Stanley said, “expectations were high going into the results” and “a lack of specific FY25 margin guide drives a sell on news and an opportunity for long-term investors.”

Walmart (WMT) dropped after forecasting lower-than-expected profit for the full year, citing an uncertain economic environment.

China’s tech stocks rallied this week, as sentiment was helped by robust earnings reports from several companies and growing optimism around artificial intelligence-driven technologies. Hong Kong-listed shares of Alibaba (BABA) rose 11% over the week, on the back of strong quarterly results, while Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY) advanced 7.7% and Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) grew 15%.

Palantir Technologies (PLTR) came in spotlight on Wednesday after a memo from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, obtained by the Washington Post, called for an 8% cut to the defense budget over each of the next five years.

Cruise operators were hammered on Thursday after Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick hinted that the tax status which favor shipping companies that fly under “flags of convenience” would end under a Trump Administration. Carnival Corporation (CCL), Royal Caribbean (RCL), and Norwegian Cruise Lines (NCLH) all closed in red.

Intel (INTC) led chips stocks higher on Tuesday after reports that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Broadcom (AVGO) could each look to acquire parts of the company. INTC advanced 14% over the week.

The Trade Desk (TTD) declined after the company topped profit expectations in fourth-quarter earnings, but missed expectations with revenue growth short of Wall Street consensus for a 25%-plus boost.

Rivian Automotive (RIVN) gained after reporting its first-ever quarterly gross profit in Q4. However, the results were partly overshadowed by a downbeat first-quarter vehicle delivery forecast.

Nikola (NKLA) saw its shares plummet over 35% after voluntarily filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in the U.S. The troubled EV maker heads into Chapter 11 with approximately $47M in cash on hand to fund the foregoing activities, implement the post-petition sale process, and exit Chapter 11 through a plan process.

Shares of Unity Software (U) jumped after its fourth quarter results beat estimates. The software company reported mixed Q4 numbers with a 25% sales drop, but the strategic portfolio sales grew 4% Y/Y.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) soared 40% on plans to offer at-home blood draws and whole-body testing through its platform with the acquisition of at-home lab testing facility Trybe Labs. Following the acquisition, the company expects to launch at-home testing facilities through its platform over the next year.

data.title: US hints Ukraine may lose Starlink access over minerals: report
data.description:
U.S. negotiators who are pushing for access to Ukrainian critical minerals have brought up the possibility that Kyiv may lose access to Elon Musk’s Starlink satellite internet system, Reuters reported Saturday, citing three sources familiar with the matter.

According to the report, the sources said Kyiv’s access to Starlink has been mentioned in talks between Ukrainian and U.S. officials after Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy rejected a proposal initially made by U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessen last week.

Starlink is owned by Musk’s SpaceX (SPACE).

The matter was brought up again on Thursday during a meeting between Zelenskyy and U.S. special envoy Keith Kellogg, one of the sources who reportedly was briefed on the talks was quoted as saying.

The source said that Ukraine was informed it faced shutoff from Starlink immediately if a deal on critical minerals was not reached, Reuters reported.

"Ukraine runs on Starlink. They consider it their North Star," the source was quoted as saying. "Losing Starlink ... would be a massive blow."

Zelenskyy has refused White House demands for $500M in mineral wealth from the country to repay the U.S. for the aid it has received since Russia’s invasion three years ago. He said Ukraine hasn’t received specific security guarantees.

Zelensky said Friday that the sides are working on a deal, and President Donald Trump said a deal is expected soon.

Starlink, SpaceX, Ukraine’s embassy in Washington, and the U.S. Treasury and Defense departments did not immediately respond to requests from Seeking Alpha. The White House could not be reached.

data.title: Real estate stocks outperform broader markets, rise marginally
data.description:
Real estate stocks rose marginally for a week that saw investor sentiment take a hit due to weak economic data.

The University of Michigan's Consumer Sentiment Index slid in February's final reading from last month, falling below the consensus estimate.

Year-ahead inflation expectations jumped up from 3.3% last month to 4.3% this month, the highest reading since November 2023 and marking two consecutive months of unusually large increases.

The minutes of the Federal Reserve's January monetary policy committee meeting showed that policymakers wanted to see further progress on inflation before adjusting interest rates.

The week saw the benchmark S&P 500 Index fall 1.66% from last week to 6,013.13.

Meanwhile, the United States 10-Year Bond Yield fell by a further five basis points to close the week at 4.43%.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Index Sector (SP500-60) advanced 0.45% for the week to close at 265.41, while the accompanying The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) was up 0.40% to finish at 42.23.

XLRE saw inflows worth $50.61M on Tuesday and Wednesday. The ETF is trading 4% above its 200-day simple moving average. Seeking Alpha analysts continue to grade XLRE as Buy, while the Quant Rating system maintains its Hold rating.

The real estate index logged gains in three out of the four trading sessions, with a 0.80% decline seen on Friday when the Cboe Volatility Index - widely seen as Wall Street's fear and volatility gauge - soared over 16%, rising above 18 for the first time in nearly two weeks.

S&P 500 Equity Real Estate Investment Trusts rose 0.60% on a weekly basis, while S&P 500 Real Estate Management & Development fell 1.73%.

Separately, Dow Jones Equity All REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) gained 0.27% W/W.

FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs (INDEXFTSE: FNER) was down 0.67% as small cap and micro cap REITs continue to trade in red, while large cap and mid cap REITs average small gains.

"The spread between the 2025 FFO multiples of large cap REITs (17.3x) and small cap REITs (12.6x) widened in January, as multiples expanded 0.1 turns for large caps but contracted 0.9 turns for small caps," noted SA analyst Simon Bowler.

Company News

  • VICI Properties (VICI) turned in 2025 guidance for adjusted funds from operations that missed Wall Street expectations as its Q4 headline results came in mixed.
  • Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) reported Q4 FFO of $0.44, which beats by $0.04, and revenue of $1.43B (+8.3% Y/Y), which beats by $60M. The company delivered full-year 2025 revenue guidance above consensus.
  • CoStar Group (CSGP) posted Q4 GAAP EPS of $0.15, which beats by $0.02, and revenue of $709M (+10.8% Y/Y), which beats by $6M. Also, the board approved an up to $500M stock repurchase program.
  • Realty Income (O), Essex Property Trust (ESS) and Prologis (PLD) raised their dividend, while Extra Space Storage (EXR) and Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) maintained it.
  • Prologis (PLD) announced the retirement of CEO Hamid Moghadam, and the appointment of President Dan Letter as a successor.
  • Scotiabank Global Equity Research upgraded Digital Realty Trust (DLR) shares on the view that the stock's recent pullback offers an attractive entry point for investors amid still-strong demand for computational power.

Winners & Losers

CoStar Group (CSGP), having posted financial results that exceed estimates and announced stock buybacks, was the top S&P 500 real estate gainer of the week.

Realty Income (O), estimated to post higher quarterly earnings in the coming week, was the next on the list. Among the REIT subcategories, retail REITs were the top gainers.

Ironically, Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) was a laggard. The retail REIT ended the week in red as the biggest weekly loser among the S&P 500 real estate companies.

CBRE Group (CBRE) preceded in the list.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Management & Development subsector logged its third consecutive losing session this week, closing the week 1.73% lower.

Billionaire investor Barry Sternlicht said property companies and investors are “nervous” about import tariffs coming from the Trump administration.

U.S. President Donald Trump this week said he may order levies on lumber and forest products alongside potential 25% tariffs on imported cars and other items in April. Earlier this month, he imposed 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum imports into the U.S. from Canada, Mexico, Brazil, and other suppliers.

"I think the tariffs are super complicated, and you don't know what's negotiation and what the end result is," Sternlicht told Bloomberg Television on Thursday. Sternlicht is the CEO of Starwood Capital Group, a private equity firm that focuses on real estate worldwide.

"Inflationary" tariffs stand to dent work for construction companies, which use Canadian lumber, and the U.S. is already facing a housing shortage of 4M-5M units. "You're seeing it in the sentiment of the builders that they're nervous. Everyone's a little nervous," he said.

Here is a look at the subsector performances for the week:

data.title: Palantir has warned about this week’s selloff.
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) fell ~15% this week after a news report about a memo from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth indicated a potential 8% cut to the U.S. defense budget over each of the next five years.

The enterprise software company has repeatedly warned about its vulnerability to fluctuations in U.S. govt. spending in its annual filings, including in its latest 10-K filing submitted on Tuesday.

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR), which develops software platforms for government and commercial organizations to support their intel operations, recorded $2.9B in revenue in 2024, with the U.S. government accounting for a major portion of its topline.

Out of roughly $1.6B the company generated from government clients, U.S. government customers generated $1.2B of revenue for PLTR in 2024, implying ~30% YoY growth. That represented a major portion of the ~$1.9B revenue the company recorded from its home market last year.

Its reliance on U.S. defense spending has prompted PLTR management to add it to the 10-K's risk section.

“U.S. government spending levels for defense-related and other programs are uncertain and may not be sustained at the levels associated with the government fiscal year 2024,” the company wrote, adding that Trump regime’s plans to evaluate govt. spending would impact its financials.

data.title: DigitalBridge, Lufax post biggest weekly gains among financial stocks; NU, COIN fall
Trump orders CFIUS to limit Chinese investments in strategic areas
data.description:
President Donald Trump on Friday issued a memorandum ordering the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States to limit investments in strategic sectors.

The national security presidential memorandum directs CFIUS, which examines proposals from foreign companies to acquire assets in the U.S., to restrict Chinese investments in certain areas, including “technology, critical infrastructure, healthcare, agriculture, energy, raw materials, and others.”

The memo calls for the establishment of “new rules to curb the exploitation of its capital, technology, and knowledge by foreign adversaries such as China to ensure that only those investments that serve American interests are allowed.”

The administration will also “consider new or expanded restrictions” on U.S. investment in China in such sectors as semiconductors, artificial intelligence, quantum, biotechnology, and aerospace, among others to prevent U.S. funds from reaching Beijing’s “Military-Civilian Fusion (MCF) strategy.”

The White House is seeking to audit foreign companies that have U.S.-listed shares, to ensure “foreign adversary companies are ineligible for pension plan contributions.”

According to the memo, the administration wants to create a “fast track” process to spur more investment by “specified allies and partners,” and for the U.S. to expedite environmental reviews for investments of more than $1 billion.

The administration is also looking to bolster CFIUS’s authority over “greenfield” investments and real estate deals close to “sensitive facilities” and “restrict foreign adversary access to U.S. talent and operations in sensitive technologies.”

Trump earlier this month leveled 10% tariffs on Chinese imports and has threatened levies against allies, such as 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum and reciprocal tariffs against countries that have their own on US products.
